item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I ended up here via the discussion on SCP-5740, and while I think the latter executes on its political leanings much better, this is just a really solid compact SCP. Immediate +1 for the world-building of "Foundation-certified cannibal", everything after that is just gravy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm really curious if your opinions on American police have changed. There's now a mountain of evidence from this summer that America is, in fact, countries like Turkmenistan, where the cops basically have free reign to do whatever they want and can throw you in jail for smoking at the airport (or, in this case, protesting against the police).

In my city alone, dozens of protesters were violently arrested, jailed, strip-searched, and placed under restrictive bail conditions for months on nonviolent misdemeanor charges only to have the charges summarily dropped because they're unsupportable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is it weird that I honestly didn't read this as "author hates the police" until reading the comments? I just took this as being a way of playing with the phrase "police are pigs" in a similar way to the original Circe myth playing with the phrase "men are pigs". While I can see the reading upon being alerted to it, and can understand the discourse related to it, I really appreciate this as just a modern retelling of the Circe myth, and upvoted it for that reason.
I do think clarifying that the SCP has paranormal powers of seduction would help explain the whole guard bit, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Finally, some ethical consumption under capitalism! +1

item.title: Re: Forensics
item.content:encoded:
Human meat presumably has a qualitatively different texture than pulled pork. A google search about the difference between human and pig meat suggests that human flesh would have a texture and appearance closer to beef or veal. I imagine a forensic scientist with the foundation's resources would also be able to examine a mass of shredded muscle and tell you what kind of animal it physically came from.

The tissue probably isn't completely identical at the genetic level to its original human state, but enough markers or sequences or whatever could still be left to identify it as belonging to a particular employee, especially if you assume the Foundation has technology not available to the public and would be tracking their employees with it just in case of something like this.

item.title: Forensics
item.content:encoded:
The package contained one pound of pulled pork that was found to be a genetic match to [missing Foundation operative].

If the tissue is genetically human, why would the Foundation call it 'pork' ? In this context, how would it be any different from a non-anomalous serial killer sending the Foundation a pound of human tissue labeled 'pork' ?

item.title: Re: +169
item.content:encoded: All the hype of +69 and +100, rolled into one for the Fourth of July!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I coincidentally just finished reading a novel about said Grecian gastronome. Easy +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All Circes Are Barbecuing!

+1 with enthusiasm

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1- Even if it stretches my suspension of disbelief, a Foundation certified cannibal is a fantastic hook.

2- Though it’s explained in the article as her being a member of IWW, Circe hating cops seems slightly odd to me, as in, I don’t expect the daughter of a Titan to deal in such mortal affairs.

3- Everyone else has already covered the guard but I’ll still mention how jarring it was for her to just fall for 4670’s escape attempt.

4- 4670 has breached containment and hasn’t been recaptured, and also killed/transformed several personnel. It seems odd she’s Euclid based on how easily she escaped.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I also want to point out that Foundation-certified cannibals don't need to be eating actual people- there are anomalies out there that produce human flesh from nothing, like that MC&D dinner plate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A neat little self-contained package with far-reaching implications, underscoring a quaint story of cannibalism and rebellion.

+40% of 2.5

item.title: STAFF POST - CLOSED
item.content:encoded:
Hi kids! We appear to have gotten into a discussion about whether or not the consumption of pork is okay that's already invoked MacNamara's Morons and prion disease. In the interests of everyone involved, I'm putting a stop to that!

This post is closed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Why don't you eat meat?" "Well you see…"

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a fine article, generally well structured and well paced, but from a moral standpoint I can't get behind it. I sympathize with the sentiments of the article and where they're coming from, but I can't really get behind this for the same reason I so adamantly reject the concept of a death penalty - the judicial (and especially extra-judicial) killing of a human being relies on the infallibility of humans when reasoning justice, and experience shows that humans aren't particularly adept at that.

Calling for the execution and then brutalization of cops isn't exactly an unpopular stance in current climates; I for one can't stand the actions of law enforcement as a whole in this country, but "law enforcement" is an institution and "cops" are human beings. While I will readily condemn institutions for their action or inaction as it pertains to the authority they hold over other people, I do not feel comfortable with others or myself casting capital judgement on a human being. I find it difficult to put myself in the headspace where I would be arrogant enough to make a capital decision regarding the life of another person, regardless of what they have or have not done, because my own reasoning weighed against the alternative of a lifetime of imprisonment leans so heavily towards the option where I am not taking the life of another human being, a decision that I cannot unmake once it has been made, regardless of what information comes out afterwards.

That all said, since the Foundation isn't sitting here and just letting her run wild it definitely sounds like they're not making some sort of moral call on this one; they're just doing their jobs, per the usual. Plenty of other articles have plenty of entities that do things for a number of reasons, some shitty and others less shitty, but that's not really a canon issue with the Foundation. One of the comments above this seems to imply the Foundation is complicit in this? But that doesn't track at all. That much doesn't seem unusual.

I'm going to upvote this because it's a good article and while the response of the guard that gets pigged is definitely jarring, it's one blemish on an otherwise fine article. My qualms with the entity are less of an issue with killing cops and more of an issue with killing anyone, for any reason. That's why I've always rejected the notion of D-Class getting terminated after they're done doing whatever they need to do. Deaths of subjects out of necessity due to well-understood containment requirements that protect many more people are distasteful but at least necessary, regardless of how much it churns my stomach. Executions by fallible executioners who have other options deeply unsettle me. I know there is scum in this world that deserves to burn for the things they've done, but locking them in a box to rot until they eventually die naturally takes the burden of fallible reasoning away from those fallible executioners, and accomplishes the same thing.

tl;dr: good article

edit: a word

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Do you actually think that raging on a creepypasta discussion page is going to change anyone's opinion?

Or that calling those you disagree with brain damaged will win anyone over to your point of view?

This is neither the forum, the context, nor a productive strategy for discussing the complex and emotionally fraught topics of justice system corruption and the relative morality of social reform strategies.

Might I suggest adjourning to Twitter? There you will also not convince anyone, but at least your vitriol may gain kudos from a social echo chamber.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You misunderstand! I meant that you’re hallucinating — most of the comments refer to the quirkiness of the “foundation-certified cannibal” or express their discontent with my stance on cops and outright change their vote as a response. Only a few people in the comments are willing to express and defend their discontent with the police institution in the United States.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
systemic levels of corruption require systemic solutions, not murders of individuals, most of whom are not guilty, for the sake of, i don't know, some kind of catharsis.

in fact, even making very thorough investigations to make sure only people who are guilty of death sentence level crimes, which little miss self-insert has not at any point been implied to do, will obviously not solve any problem BECAUSE THE PROBLEM IS SYSTEMIC BY DEFINITION. It's #resistance stuff. My next SCP: the epic girl that turns the orange man into an orange, thus fixing American imperialism instantly. The conclusion is that the entire O5 council looks into the camera and says that it's good.

a good response to prison abolition is: what the fuck do we do with the actual bad guys, the serial murderers and rapists who refuse to be reformed and immediately commit more crimes no matter what? such as, oh, a serial cannibal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: actually if you think that killing and eating people is good that's a you problem, and a very severe one. Killing and eating people is evil, and people don't really just commit evil because it's evil.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. It is tied to the Circe mythos, and fairly extensively at that. Cici's last name hints at her father being Helios, she's a seductress who turns men into pigs, and she has a green thumb!
2. A fair number of people have mentioned this point, and that's fair. The implication is that she could only turn people into pigs by touching the tops of their unprotected heads, but the Foundation couldn't figure this part out until she actually got the chance to do it. I'll go ahead and make that clear.
3. Yes.
4. That's a you problem.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. The two ideas aren't mutually exclusive.
  2. That's a damn good point.
  3. 'solely to prevent the aforementioned incident', I suppose. And good point too.
  4. Because people are angry at institutionalized systemic levels of corruption and brutality from police.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this is bad.

1) could benefit a lot by tying it to, like, the Circe mythos as opposed to "cops = pigs XD ebin" repeated like 50 times
2) is everyone staffed in this site a moron? do they not brief the guards? "skin contact with this object literally turns people into pigs" should be an absolute defense against any promises to get laid. Do guards not have photo ID? If the object has the ability to actually affect people's brains at a distance, why is this not mentioned at any point?
3) why on earth would you need asexual guards? quite a few asexual people can feel sexual attraction albeit at a reduced level, and certainly a lot of them still have sex. is this solely to prevent the aforementioned incident?
you know what could help instead? the buddy system. or not recruiting the guards out of the McNamara program as they apparently do.
4) does anyone in the comments have prion disease? am i hallucinating? what is this? why do 90% of the people pretend that she's a good person and tell off everyone else extremely condescendingly? hello?
she kills and eats people. are you confused about the idea that killing and eating people is bad? in what other ways is your moral system lacking?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That bitch of a girl need to go to prison and get killed. Girl luring people especially men into her and turn them into PIGS! Like whaaat. And people just wanted to eat some pork just became a cannibal with absolutely no intention at all. By the way, good SCP. The writing is like a police reports blending with scientific reports. Good job. +1 for you. o(> w <)o

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When I read the article first time, I thought it was OK. Then after reading through the discussion, I don't really like it anymore. Maybe it's because I never had a negative experience with a cop (Or any experience for that matter) so I can't really relate with anyone who hates cops. I'm just going to hold on to my vote as a no and maybe wait for a response.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
two other certified cannibals.

Love it. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not sure if intentional, but the mention of "electroshock weapon" brought up images of cattle prods. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shouldn't that be specified explicitly somewhere in the description? Since they know it enough to change the containment procedures to account for it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I upvoted at "A foundation certified cannibal". Yes, the very first line. I haven't even read the rest yet I just wanted to comment that that opener made me chuckle.

Edit: Not so keen on the whole 'fuck cops' message that you the author seem to have, but I can understand why the character in the scip had that philosophy so it didn't detract.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sometimes it really is just that simple. +1
i think i may have commented about this already, but i had to come back bc this is literally standard scip lore to me now it’s fucking great. staple article lol

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
wtf i love circe now?????

+1(312)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. It seems pretty clear that Circe’s reasons for targeting police stem from her political leanings and mythological precedent. I don’t need to push some implied-false narrative that police are bad when their racial profiling, heightened rates of domestic violence, blue wall of silence, absurdly high rate of murder compared to a risk of death lower than a truck driver, and more indicate that said narrative is true.

2. As part of her ability to lure people, Circe is implied to be anomalously seductive — hence why the Foundation starts using asexual guards.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This didn't do anything for me, which I would normally no-vote since it is well-written, but I ended up downvoting it for the following reasons:

1. It's just Circe played straight, but she hates police. If there was a reason for her to hate police, this wouldn't rub me the wrong way, but it seems like you're just pushing the "Police Bad" narrative that news companies like to shove down their listeners' collective throats.

2. She escaped by seducing a trained guard. This wouldn't work on an overpaid grunt at a County Jail, much less trained personnel for a top-secret international paranormal organization. I could live with the X-Man Syndrome since that was written well here, but I would highly suggest changing this. I don't think the Foundation would employ a guard who is so incompetent as to have been seduced by an anomaly that she knows can turn her into a pork-chop.

Like I said, the writing itself isn't bad, but the contents of that writing don't add up for me. Just my two cents, and everyone else seems to enjoy this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This makes things a lot more clear, so I'm appreciative of the answer, though I laughed more at the thought of the Foundation needing cannibals in this post than in the skip. But I'm really pleased with the explanation that the Foundation is overtly authoritarian in this because it recontextualizes everything else and makes it make sense.

I really want to see a Foundation Cannibalistic Division now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The simplest explanation is that this skip is an anarchist Circe, who emigrated from at Greece at some point in the past and has been using her ability to turn people into pigs for praxis.

Why would the Foundation have certified cannibals? Who knows? In this case, it’s because it’s cheaper to have a cannibal first make sure that some random barbecue pork is worth investigating further instead of subjecting every baby-back rib they find to a DNA test. More broadly, the implication is that there are skips for which the Foundation needs and is willing to train cannibals to study and/or contain. The implication is partly humorous, because it implies that the Foundation is willing to train cannibals, and partly horrifying, because it implies that they need cannibals.

As for the resistance to cognitohazards, I can probably remove the “all kinds” without unduly harming anything. It’s meant to imply that the Foundation used some less-than-ethical means to try and interrogate Circe. This version of the Foundation is decidedly authoritarian and short on ethics, to the point that Circe has identified them and is willing to treat them as fascists.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a competently-written work, so I'm not inclined to downvote. Instead, I will say that I'm genuinely confused as to what this is meant to be about. Maybe that can be chalked up to a general lack of regional, cultural, or mythological understanding on my part. And I haven't taken a proper read through the comments, so it's very possible the parts I'm confused about have been explained.

The only tonal issues I have are as follows:

+ show block

Foundation-certified cannibal

For what purpose would the Foundation staff active cannibals?

Extreme resistance to all known forms of chemical, memetic, and cognitohazardous brain-chemistry-altering substances.

It's fine that these are SCP-4670's actual properties. However, I don't see how the Foundation could determine this without doing an extended and potentially dangerous regimen of invasive medical testing. Especially when that testing involves a very real danger of being turned into a pig and the anomaly is specifically stated as being noncompliant. It just doesn't make total diegetic sense that they'd know this.

– hide block

In any case, this isn't a downvote, but it isn't an upvote either — unless someone would be willing to explain this in plain terms.

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded: We be gettin’ hyped to the sound of da police!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wanted to point out that one can understand a viewpoint without necessarily supporting it. I'm sure there are plenty of real world examples but my brain is wired to default to pop culture. Likewise, I can understand where the piece is coming from but don't agree with the sentiment. Still a solid entry though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I understand why Walter White started selling meth, but that doesn't mean that I agree with or endorse his decision.

Eh I don't think saying "fuck cops" is in any way comparable to selling narcotics…

And yeah, automobile accidents are one of the leading causes of death in most lines of work actually, which is why professions like truck driving and delivery driving are more deadly than being an officer.

+ show block
– hide block

I also agree that as far as western countries go, this is definitely an American-specific issue due to how gung-ho and militarized the American police has become as of late. They have all of these toys, but not the training that military operators receive to go along with them.

SWAT teams will breach and clear, but they will also scream commands at people who just got flashbanged, and then shoot them when they don't listen. Officers will chase a suspect down, knock the wind out of them, handcuff them, and then kneel on their lower back, causing them to suffocate via positional asphyxia (like what happened to Eric Garner a few years back). This stuff happens because they don't know better. Positional asphyxia isn't exactly common knowledge, and not many people have been flashbanged before.

There's also a systemic cultural issue with police forces that they self-govern, often very poorly. Things like "Rule 303" and sentiments like "We write the reports", alongside inflated police death statistics (having a heart attack in your squadcar is not a workplace related death if you ask me) and the war on drugs serve to escalate the situation and make it seem as if there is a war against the police when in reality, police deaths are at a 50 year low (see my previous sources for this). Couple this with the fact that the problem cops watch out for each other, Courts have determined time and time again that cops do not actually have a duty to protect the lives of US citizens, and the cops that call out injustices get ostracized or bullied into silence, and well, you have a fairly toxic culture on your hands.

Basically, without major cultural shift and reform, things will probably get worse before they get better.

item.title: OH HELL YEAH
item.content:encoded: That's a good fucking skip right there. I'm absolutely in love with this whole thing. There's so much subtlety that ties everything together so well. I'm especially a fan of the description of the thing's taste. On top of that, It's short, sweet and obvious enough to get on the first time through, but has enough threads to pick up more on the way back around.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now, I won't go around yelling "fuck cops", but, with a little bit of context, its a bit easier to understand why people do.

I understand why Walter White started selling meth, but that doesn't mean that I agree with or endorse his decision.

I don't want to get too off-topic, but I think it's more a problem with the American system than with cops. I wonder if this is an American issue, or if other developed countries deal with this issue as well.1

Full disclosure though: my dad was a traffic cop for thirty years before he retired, and one of my earliest memories is of him coming home from the hospital after some drunk moron hit him with a car.

Footnotes
1. Excluding countries like Turkmenistan, of course, where the cops basically have free reign to do whatever they want and can throw you in jail for smoking at the airport (which almost happened to my uncle, true story).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
most people that are shot by cops are either resisting arrest or pulling a gun out of their pants so I would say its justified.

A source on that would be appreciated :)
It also should be noted that "resisting arrest" can include things like not obeying the conflicting commands of two or more officers, not complying to the commands of a SWAT team after a flashbang just blew out your hearing, running as the direct result of a fight, flight, or freeze instinct, and so on. Either way, "resisting arrest" should not be an executable offense in my opinion.

And anyways, I like to think that respect is earned. With their current track record, I think it's mostly irrelevant whether or not they are a "good cop" or a "bad cop". They don't have enough training to properly protect human life given the amount of firepower they wield. The motto was supposed to be "protect and serve", not "kill more civilians than the US military". I would say the respect has not been earned, and it certainly is not reciprocated by police.

Now, I won't go around yelling "fuck cops", but, with a little bit of context, its a bit easier to understand why people do. In your original comment, you expressed your confusion at this logic. Hopefully this helps a bit. Cheers!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What you said is right but my point still stands, saying "fuck cops" is just disrespectful, because there are a lot of good cops out there who are doing their jobs.
And besides, most people that are shot by cops are either resisting arrest or pulling a gun out of their pants so I would say its justified.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So that means all police are bad

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, considering that being a cop is actually less dangerous than being a truck driver by a crazy wide margin, I don't think walking around with their fingers on the trigger like they could get shot at every traffic stop is justified. Cops are not trained well enough to utilize the sheer amount of deadly force that they do, and too many innocent people die because of it.

Whether you agree with the political standpoint or not, it's pretty clear that American police specifically need to be better trained and they need sweeping top down reform because the current statistics are unacceptable.

The bottom line is that cops have killed 1147 people in the US in 2017. 128 cops died in the line of duty that same year. That's almost 9 times the number of people killed by cops compared to cops killed by work related injuries that same year. US cops have actually killed more than double the number of US citizens in 2017 than US Troops have killed civilians in the same year.

That is straight up wrong, no bones about it. The police are more of a danger to the people than the people have ever been to the police.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dr. ████████ ██████

The number of characters here is a reference to Dr. Hannibal Lecter right? +1 from me!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really don't get the logic behind this:
- We should learn not to discriminate and be happy.
- Fuck cops just because they're cops.
I get that some cops are bad people but ( and I know this has been said a lot ) not all cops are evil pigs who just don't care about other people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I want to like this more than I like this. On one hand, it's extremely clever in it's unsubletly and I'm all for more mythology on the site. I love the conflation/update of Circe hating cops. On the other, I'm getting very spiteful vibes from the whole thing and I'm not a fan. I almost want there to be notes about how the cops in question were under investigation for <insert Bad Stuff here> but that would ruin the moral ambiguity that I like about the character here.

Hesitant novote, with one buttcheek on the upvote side of the fence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is utterly delightful in how bizare it is, especially in the first half. Excellent use of vocabulary and language kept this highly engaging. However, I have to say that everything in the addendum feels like dead weight that's repeating what we were already told earlier in the article with more detail, but without actually adding anything to the story. The Foundation-certified cannibal was a cool little thing to have in the containment procedures but leaning into them in the discovery without adding any new real information about them leaves the reader wondering.

I would have honestly upvoted this and considered it an all-time great without the discovery log, but even with it I still find this to be an easy upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Tbh as someone who was mugged at gun point as a teen outside of a bar filled with cops. When I reported this to them no one really moved. They never found the dude. I would’ve gotten as much help had I made the report to God.

Not all work has to be subtle, also.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: why do you think i booed "Holy moly".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why are you and I the only ones who realized this is

+ Show whatever
- Hide whatever

Circe from the Odyssey?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You make a good point re: why ace guards specifically. I’ve tweaked the article accordingly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dude FUCK cops, ammirite? That is, until I'm the victim of a crime and need law enforcement to help me!
On a serious note, I dislike this because it wasn't even trying to be subtle. Too on the nose for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Happy Pride. Fuck cops.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really wanted to like this. It had a cool concept and the writing is good, but it lacks too much in substance in favor of political virtue signaling. From the asexual guard (why not just say no male staff, like SCP-166?) to the intense cop hate (I've no love for authority figures, but just "fuck cops" as a premise doesn't make it good) to the IWW membership to help signify what a "woke" person they are) this just feels like a grandstand instead of a "real" SCP. It's definitely a -1 because if you take away the sentiment fluff it's basically just a soap box article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You had me at “Foundation certified cannibal”. This officer upvotes hard +1

item.title: +1 comin' straight from tha underground.
item.content:encoded:
It took me way longer than I want to admit to understand what was going on here after reading the whole line about the "several dozen sets of uniforms, equipment, wallets, badges, and other belongings of missing police officers dating back to the 1980s".

Good shit ARD, good shit.

item.title: Re: +69
item.content:encoded: BE GAY DO CRIME

item.title: Re: Pork Chop Praxis
item.content:encoded: Love this, can i also point out that, "Foundation-certified cannibal Dr. ████████ ██████ during his first visit to Cici's Pit Bar-B-Q" the redacted doctor, i'm fairly certain that the name ties to Hannibal Lecter. which is lovely if it is.
